summary: Stancil argues that “self-serve analytics” isn’t defined by features or who/what answers questions, but by how the experience feels to the people using and providing it. Because trust, comfort, and skill differ by user and context, the same tool can feel self-serve to some and not to others, so teams should design for the experience that makes their users feel at home rather than chase a canonical feature checklist.
money_quotes: Defining self-serve isn’t about knowing when we see it, but knowing when we feel it.
The experience is what matters, not the functionality.
If they don’t like the experience or don’t trust the answer it gives them—regardless of how much the tool meets some abstract definition of self-serve—they’ll just ask someone for help.
That’s why it’s dangerous to define business users as a vague, homogenous unit.
So often, as data teams, we chase the self-serve experience that we think we’re supposed to build.
key_insight: Self-serve analytics succeeds when users feel comfortable and trust the experience, not when a tool checks a feature list.
